comments.author: Child, Nicholas
comments.body:
yes this can happen if a large amount of memory is already pinned. We only pin RLIMIT / num_user_contexts / 4  (so hfi1 only ping 25% of memory total).
I have tried digging into this before and could not reproduce, saw plenty of other errors like those in bug report:
Some runs show the OPX_TID_CACHE assert.

Some runs “passed residuals”

Some runs die due to NODE failure.
Someone mentioned HPL by design allocates 90% of system mem so I don’t know what expected behavior is (you use all the memory and things fail… what are we expecting?). Have not been told what exactly the “bug” is.

comments.author: McCrory, Duane
comments.body:
This is just an FYI. I ran the latest FM on phkpemb002 (thanks Paul for installing this) in simulation with packet debugging enabled and got a log and put it here: /nfs/shares/jiralrg/STL-76651/plat3FmNew1.log. I didn’t notice any anomalies.The latest L8sim code has some improvements with MAD packet decode and display of MCTXT messages, so it make it a lot easier to follow along with what the FM and driver are doing in the log (I suggest just doing a search in your editor with “AID=”)
Prior to starting up simulation, I went in and modified the /etc/opa-fm/opafm.xml file to turn off the PM, since it still defaults on in FM collateral.
  <!-- Common PM (Performance Manager) attributes -->
  <Pm>
    <!-- ESM does not support Start via XML configuration. Use CLI commands -->
    <Start>0</Start> <!-- default PM startup for all instances -->
I started simulation up like this: 
[root@phkpemb002 jkr]# ./plat3.sh PORT_MANAGER_DEBUG 2 DEBUG 0x80300000
Once the simulation is up, I start the driver by doing:
.driveronly
And then to start the FM I do:
.fmstart
You will also see “.portbounce 1” and “.portbounce 2” in the log, where I was manually bouncing ports. Port 1 is the port that the FM is running “through” and is connected to port 13 on MYR. Port 2 is the other JKR port, is is connected to port 15 on MYR.

comments.author: McCrory, Duane
comments.body:
I did some more review of the simulation logs.
The one anomaly I did notice, is that the FM is programming the Linear Forwarding Table after the Link goes INIT when its own link to the fabric comes back up (see the .portbounce 1 sequence), and then it reprograms it again after the link goes active.
When the non-FM port is bounced (.portbounce 2), the FM does remove the impacted port from the Linear formwarding table when the Link goes down, and only adds it after the link goes back active.
Also given what I’m seeing in the traces, the amount of MAD packets that occur before a port is removed from the Linear Forwarding Table is significant, making me realize we probably should put MYR firmware change in to automatically remove ports from the Linear and Multicast routing tables when a port goes down.
I need to do some checking regarding the Multicast forwarding table.

comments.body: The data corruption I saw was with NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL=SYS and specific to the external DMABUF path.  The PR starts to address that; however, it needs some additional investigation to ensure it is a complete solution.  James/Gustavo are running without NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL=SYS and seeing data validation errors with both internal and external DMABUF.  I am able to reproduce on our development boxes now that I know the difference and I am investigating more there.

comments.body:
can customer please confirm that the nodes involved in the problem are using the OPXS hfi1? They should run the following commands:

And capture the full output of those two commands in files; I suggest they store their output with the commands before the output one per node involved in the problem. They should then provide those files to us. They should label those files or the output within a file with the node name as well.
By comparing the srcversion: field from modinfo hfi1 to /sys/module/hfi1/srcversion, we can confirm that the nodes are running 1) the same driver and 2) that driver comes from OPXS. This is necessary to make sure we (driver developers) are looking at the correct code.

comments.author: Cunningham, Brendan
comments.body:
13 February 2026 mmstor19 hang-break observations
Have looked at the data from 13 February 2026 GPFS problems involving mmstor13, mmstor19, mmstor21.
Michael provided hfi1stats data captured from these nodes in 1s intervals for 60m (3600s) leading up to the hang-break on mmstor19. Data covers nodes {mmstor10,mmstor12,mmstor13,mmstor17,mmstor19,mmstor21,mmstor25}.
As explained by Michael, in this hang-break case, mmstor19 is the GPFS node that hang-broke. All other nodes were communicating with mmstor19.
Data ends when mmstor19 is detected as hung-broken and script reboots it. Of particular interest is the last 8s of data here.
Observations:

Discussion
RcDupRew and RcResend events in last 8s to me suggests that RDMA communication at least between the QPs used by GPFS broke. That mmstor19 “thinks” it is receiving duplicate packets and all other mmstor* nodes communicating with mmstor19 “think” that their packets are not being acknowledged.
It could be that the RcDupRew/RcResend events are only the final symptom of an earlier problem. But if this is the case, it’s the most concrete symptom we have to go on here to work backwards to a root cause.
I propose a fairly simple chain of logical conditions to explain this:

comments.author: Cunningham, Brendan
comments.body:
I’d like the customer to enable hfi1 tracepoints and collect trace data from mmstor* nodes when a hang break occurs.
Here is a script to enable tracepoints/trace events on one node:
#!/bin/bash
# Enable trace events for hfi1-GPFS problem
set -xe
cd /sys/kernel/tracing/

# Clear existing trace ring
echo > trace
# Disable existing trace events
echo 0 > events/enable
# Enable our hfi1_rc trace events
echo 1 > events/hfi1_rc/hfi1_ack/enable
echo 1 > events/hfi1_rc/hfi1_rcv_error/enable
echo 1 > events/hfi1_rc/hfi1_rc_ack_do/enable
echo 1 > events/hfi1_tid/hfi1_sender_reset_psn/enable

# Make sure tracing is on
echo 1 > tracing_on

# Now run your reproducer and 'cat /sys/kernel/tracing/trace'
# This'll dump the trace ring and clear the contents.
This script and all commands working with the trace ring must be run as root.
To collect the trace events from the trace ring when the problem occurs, do cat /sys/kernel/tracing/trace > /root/somefile. This will read but not clear the trace ring.
If possible, run this on all mmstor* nodes. When the hang-break or RcDupRew events occur, capture /sys/kernel/tracing/trace from all nodes as close to the time that the event of interest as possible. The trace ring is, as its name implies, a ring buffer and so when it fills up, older events will be overwritten.
The data in /sys/kernel/tracing/trace is in memory and is not saved to disk at reboot, so make sure that whatever collection+reboot script dumps /sys/kernel/tracing/trace from the hang-broken node before rebooting it.

I found the (copied from efa) comment but it doesn’t seem the likely culprit.

	/* Both Open MPI (and possibly other MPI implementations) and
	 * Libfabric use the same live binary patching to enable memory
	 * monitoring, but the patching technique only allows a single
	 * "winning" patch.  The Libfabric memhooks monitor will not
	 * overwrite a previous patch, but instead return
	 * -FI_EALREADY.  There are three cases of concern, and in all
	 * but one of them, we can avoid changing the default monitor.
	 *
	 * (1) Upper layer does not patch, such as Open MPI 4.0 and
	 * earlier.  In this case, the default monitor will be used,
	 * as the default monitor is either not the memhooks monitor
	 * (because the user specified a different monitor) or the
	 * default monitor is the memhooks monitor, but we were able
	 * to install the patches.  We will use the default monitor in
	 * this case.
	 *
	 * (2) Upper layer does patch, but does not export a memory
	 * monitor, such as Open MPI 4.1.0 and 4.1.1.  In this case,
	 * if the default memory monitor is not memhooks, we will use
	 * the default monitor.  If the default monitor is memhooks,
	 * the patch will fail to apply, and we will change the
	 * requested monitor to UFFD to avoid a broken configuration.
	 * If the user explicitly requested memhooks, we will return
	 * an error, as we can not satisfy that request.
	 *
	 * (3) Upper layer does patch and exports a memory monitor,
	 * such as Open MPI 4.1.2 and later.  In this case, the
	 * default monitor will have been changed from the memhooks
	 * monitor to the imported monitor, so we will use the
	 * imported monitor.
	 *
	 * The only known cases in which we will not use the default
	 * monitor are Open MPI 4.1.0/4.1.1.
	 *
	 * It is possible that this could be better handled at the
	 * mem_monitor level in Libfabric, but so far we have not
	 * reached agreement on how that would work.
	 *
	 * Copied from efa provider
	 */

The state dumps show the activation of the links, followed by some HOQ Discards (which need to be understood separately, for performance reasons), followed by a eventRbufIntStatus0RbufCntrlSbe, 27 hours after the links were activated. There events were reported on two different ports, ports 89 and 61, separated by roughly an hour. The trace only reports the first occurrence of these failures, but MYR has a counter that counts these events
 myr->.rporterrorcounters
  rbuf_bk2_sbe_cnt                    =                   8  d0066a0106000265:P61<->P29:d0066a0106000241 FULLY_RETIMED
  rbuf_bk2_sbe_cnt                    =                   3  d0066a0106000265:P89<->P02:d0066a010100085d PASSIVE_COPPER
The Rbuf memory is divided into four banks, numbered 0-3. Interestingly, all of the errors seem to be occurring on bank 2. Looking back through the historical data, bank 2 is the only one that ever reports this error. This is a compelling clue, but I don't know what it means yet.
Zooming in on the linkmgrtrace to when these events occurred, we see the first event and the presumed downstream effects (packet length errors being detected by Tport).
Port 89 SBE event and downstream effects
  1   544   101974312433     7657257873 p=89 phy=93 eventRbufIntStatus0RbufCntrlSbe            0x0001(1)
  1   545   101974312438              5 p=91 phy=89 eventTportIntStatus0TportShrtPkt           0x0001(1)
  1   546   101974312438              0 p=91 phy=89 eventTportIntStatus0TportPktEBP            0x0001(1)
Port 61 SBE event and downstream effects (just under an hour after the port 89 event)
  1   556   105392239637     1167919621 p=61 phy=69 eventRbufIntStatus0RbufCntrlSbe            0x0001(1)
  1   557   105392239703             66 p=7  phy=21 eventTportIntStatus0TportShrtPkt           0x0001(1)

  1   565   128568245349     5091325081 p=91 phy=89 eventTportIntStatus0TportLongPkt           0x0001(1)
The downstream effects that occurred after the port 61 event may or may not be correlated to port 61 - they could be additional occurrences of the original failing port 89. Since firmware is not clearing the interrupt that identifies the SBE, we don't know the correlation of downstream effects to failing Rports. However, The Rport error counter tells us that there were 3 events for port 89 and 8 events for port 61, so it is likely that the downstream effects observed after the port 61 event were in fact associated with the port 61 SBE.
The other noteworthy thing that is revealed by this trace is what happens immediately after the long packet is detected on port 91 at trace entry 565. This long packet exposes the MYR ASIC bug that misplaces the EBP, which causes port 91 to go down to due to a PKT_TOO_LONG error detected by its link partner (see the earlier trace). 
The port 91 link eventually comes back up and immediately logs new packet length errors on Tport:

comments.author: Murphy, Philip
comments.body:
If we are using 10KB MTU, then maybe the issue is related to a suboptimal solution to .   This issue in that Jira is that rarely we could end up in a position in which we used most of the buffers in one RBUF bank and very few in the other.  To accommodate, we had to ensure that all outstanding credits could fit into one of the two banks.  A bank can hold 4096 credits and to accept its half of an 8KB packet, we need enough room for 4KB credits.  Since there are 64 bytes per credit, this means we need 64 extra credits, so the global credit setting is 4096 - 64 = 4032.  However, if we have a 10KB (10240B) packet, then we actually need to reserve room for 10240/2 bytes = 5120 bytes / 64 bytes/credit = 80.  So we should have set the global credits to 4016 (or maybe 4014 if two are unusable).  
Today if we get a 10KB packet while one of the banks is nearly full (and the corollary is the other is nearly empty), the SOP check passes because we have some free buffers (but not 80).  We end up with a mid-packet failure when we run out of credits and probably truncate with EBP.   Not sure if this can eventually cause a PKT_TOO_LONG error.

comments.author: Murphy, Philip
comments.body:
I think I have a complete picture now what happens downstream of the pointer exhaustion.  The root cause is a rogue SRAM write triggered by mid-packet pointer exhaustion in the RBUF. When the RBUF bank23 pointer pool runs dry during a large packet write, the hardware does not stop writing. Instead, it forces EOP+EBP control flags and writes them to a stale SRAM address — an address belonging to a different, valid packet already stored in RBUF. This corrupts the victim packet's data, and if the TPORT is simultaneously reading that victim credit, the dual-port SRAM produces a same-address read-write collision yielding the observed SBE.
The condition is enabled by ASIC-4970: the RBUF's two bank-pair pointer pools (bank01, bank23) can fill unevenly, and the global credit limit of 4032 leaves insufficient headroom (62 free entries vs. 80 needed for a 10KB MTU packet) to prevent mid-packet exhaustion of the smaller pool.
Every symptom in the JIRA — RbufCntrlSbe, TportShrtPkt, TportPktEBP, TportLongPkt, PKT_TOO_LONG link-down, pktDropPending — is directly explained by this mechanism.  Changing the global credits from 4032 to 4014 should eliminate the root cause.

E-mail draft:  MVAPICH + OPX: GPU buffer MR descriptor required for HMEM support (613.soma_s failure
Hello,
We are hitting a segfault when running the SPEChpc 613.soma_s benchmark with MVAPICH plus 4.1 built with CUDA 12.9 + OPX (libfabric) on our hardware. The exception occurs during an MPI_Allreduce with MPI_IN_PLACE on a GPU device buffer.
OPX requires FI_MR_HMEM — when a buffer resides in GPU device memory, the application (or MPI middleware) must provide a valid memory region descriptor (desc) to libfabric send/recv calls so that OPX can identify the buffer's memory type (CUDA, ZE, etc.) and handle it appropriately. We are observing that MVAPICH passes desc=NULL for these GPU buffers. Without the descriptor, OPX defaults to treating the pointer as host memory, and the code segfaults attempting to pin a GPU device pointer as system memory.What we've tested:

Both MPICH and Open MPI correctly provide an MR descriptor for GPU buffers when using OPX, and the same application runs successfully.Can someone please help investigate why MVAPICH is not registering the GPU buffers with fi_mr_regattr() to pass the resulting descriptor to fi_tsend() / fi_trecv() and related calls?  This is required by the libfabric API for providers that set FI_MR_HMEM.
Please let us know if you have any questions or need additional details to reproduce the issue.

comments.author: Wilkins, Michael
comments.body: Hi   , the recently proposed solution championed by the performance team is to use a PMPI intercept on Open MPI to performance our own allreduce implementation. My prototype code is HERE, and the initial performance data is very good (>3x performance improvement vs. Open MPI alone, ~90% of the performance of the NDR for the Soma application).We would appreciate your feedback. I am continuing to optimize the code to further improve the performance and preparing the repository for immediate public release ( ).

comments.author: Bollig, Robert
comments.body:
Hi Michael, is the plan to post a pull request with openMPI to make this custom allreduce kernel a  feature of the OFI (non-UCC) branch? If it is a hacky prototype it might be OK as a demonstrator during acceptance, but it will not suffice during production when control of the active openMPI stack moves out of our hands.
Overall this is a step in the right direction. Ideally we should push for merging the following pull request  as it seems to be abandoned. We could resurrect it and enable better OFI allreduce for the UEC provider as well.

comments.author: Bollig, Robert
comments.body: This work is very important in the short term and long term. Short term because this is a benchmark that has been flagged as a potential issue by NEC and CN Sales for RWTH acceptance close to a year ago. Long term because we simply need to have GPU performance/feature parity with UCX in order to win over Nvidia customers. Especially when the barrier to parity is merely man hours, rather than legal/closed-source issues with Nvidia drivers.

comments.author: Wilkins, Michael
comments.body: Yes, the plan is to evaluate options to implement GPU allreduce kernels in Open MPI once we overcome this initial hurdle. Not sure if we will be able to resurrect Joseph’s work as-is since its quite old, and lots has changed within Open MPI since then. We are also exploring our own collectives library like UCC.

comments.author: Bollig, Robert
comments.body: That’s great! Effectively except allreduce/reduce there aren’t many MPI operations that perform any compute, but the variety of collective algorithms and possible mathematical operations in that alone is already substantial.Would a GPU vendor agnostic language like Kokkos be useful to create kernels that work on both nvidia and AMD?

comments.author: Wilkins, Michael
comments.body: Its a good question. In reality, I think the kernels are relatively small and simple, so duplicating for AMD + NVIDIA isn’t too much work. My concern using a compatibility layer like Kokkos is it would require customers to also support it in their environment. I would be curious for your opinion on whether that could be an issue.

comments.author: Akella, Sreesa
comments.body:
I was talking to  yesterday and I also mentioned this to  already. I see the tport packet drop pending issue we are seeing in our local cluster is a different scenario than what we are seeing TACC. This is something that is caused due to our manual link bounces/disables on the ports during regular traffic whereas the link bounce at TACC is happening due to PKT_TOO_LONG error. However, what is happening subsequent to that at TACC could be what is happening in the local cluster where the FM is coming in and configuring or changing things around the time Tport is busy processing those excess of VL0 packet tags that arrive while it is in Link Init and tport getting into bad state.
So, for tackling that specific issue we could use a separate link teardown recipe where we backpressure the Rports from sending any more tags while we are link teardown and hold the backpressure all the way up until the link goes fully active. The changes to link teardown recipe would be the following:

 and  have been trying to replicate the scenario and try this recipe out in DV already. However  can try to implement this in a local branch and we can try it out with our local cluster tests to see how it goes.
Secondarily, the PKT_TOO_LONG at TACC is possibly causing us Tport to be in bad state even before we get to a link bounce state as it might be stuck waiting for a packet to complete while the link bounce happens. For handling this case we might need the PKT_DROP_PENDING WA in place to get the Tport out of this stuck state for that specific packet (not loop for all the tags/packets). After this we can get the link out of teardown normally without getting stuck with lot of tags pending in tag buffer while we reset the tport. Post link teardown to avoid the above issue of tags coming in during link init we would need the above link teardown recipe updates too.
We can try the PKT_DROP_PENDING WA recipe subsequently when we can replicate the PKT_TOO_LONG scenario in our local cluster.

comments.author: McCrory, Duane
comments.body:
In simulation, even without a job running, when I bounce an hfi port,  I do see some “unexpected” data VL packets being generated by the hfi prior to the link going active. I’ve seen simulation discard the packets for different reasons:1)  an invalid output result from the URT lookup of the DLID (i.e. 0x7f in the URT table for the dlid)2) bad link state for a data VL packet (i.e. the port is LINK_INIT)It made me wonder, if the tportPktDropPending issue relates to packets being received on the rport that we are bouncing.My original goal of using simulation was so that I could gather some tport/rport CSR write tracing information for Sreesa, and I’ve done that. Below are some notes about what is traced. I’m attaching the CSR trace data to the JIRA.In the trace the links come up to Init, then I do a “.driveronly” to start up the hfi1 driver, and then I follow that by starting the fm with “.fmstart”Along with the rport and tport CSR WR trace data, you will see DIAG2 messages for all the MAD packets (for all ports), but you can identify which CSRs are being written for a MAD packet look something like:
myr(0) DIAG2 00000085 0100000d                   swTask::0xb002159c smaInvokeAidHandler+44 pc=0xb00215c8 CCOUNT=1136002636 USEC=1367025
myr(0) P13 CPORT CSR WR CHN 0x0b080280:=0x0000000000000000; // TPORT_SC_TO_VL_MAP[0]              swTask::0xb001ecb4 smaFncScVlNtMappingTable+240
myr(0) P13 CPORT CSR WR CHN 0x0b080288:=0x0000000000000000; // TPORT_SC_TO_VL_MAP[1]              swTask::0xb001ecb4 smaFncScVlNtMappingTable+240
myr(0) P13 CPORT CSR WR CHN 0x0b080290:=0x0000000000000000; // TPORT_SC_TO_VL_MAP[2]              swTask::0xb001ecb4 smaFncScVlNtMappingTable+240
myr(0) P13 CPORT CSR WR CHN 0x0b080298:=0x000000000000000f; // TPORT_SC_TO_VL_MAP[3]              swTask::0xb001ecb4 smaFncScVlNtMappingTable+240
myr(0) P13 CPORT CSR WR CHN 0x0b0802a0:=0x0000000000000000; // TPORT_SC_TO_VL_MAP[4]              swTask::0xb001ecb4 smaFncScVlNtMappingTable+240
myr(0) P13 CPORT CSR WR CHN 0x0b0802a8:=0x0000000000000000; // TPORT_SC_TO_VL_MAP[5]              swTask::0xb001ecb4 smaFncScVlNtMappingTable+240
myr(0) P13 CPORT CSR WR CHN 0x0b0802b0:=0x0000000000000000; // TPORT_SC_TO_VL_MAP[6]              swTask::0xb001ecb4 smaFncScVlNtMappingTable+240
myr(0) P13 CPORT CSR WR CHN 0x0b0802b8:=0x0000000000000000; // TPORT_SC_TO_VL_MAP[7]              swTask::0xb001ecb4 smaFncScVlNtMappingTable+240

The components of the DIAG2 output here are the AID, and the attribute modifier (attmod). In this case the AID=0x85 and the attmod is the port number (0xd), and you can see it must be ScVlNtMappingTable MAD based on the CSR writes that happen after it.

comments.author: Rothermel, Brent
comments.body:

We reproduced this several times. we took a state dump after the hang and placed it here:

comments.author: Rothermel, Brent
comments.body:
I forgot to mention an important detail in our testing yesterday. One of the things we wanted to try and do was simplify the test where we were bouncing a single ISL and it was the only path out of the switch. So we disabled all of the ISLs coming out of the left side of the tier 3 switch except for port 1. We then bounced port 1 and then check for a hang. We bounce the port five times and we're unable to make it hang. This was the point where we re enabled port 3 and saw the failure I described in my earlier post today.
A week or two ago, Dan Magee and I did a similar test where we bounced many ISLs concurrently. When we did this, we were unable to make the failure happen even though we ran the test all night

comments.author: Rothermel, Brent
comments.body:
We learned a couple of new things from our testing today:

We believe that the fm is updating the LFT before it has fully activated the port, causing the drops and potentially causing the hang. Tomorrow, we are going to try a custom version of the FM that allows us to pause the update of the LFT and MFT while proceeding with the port activation. We believe this will make the drops go away, and we will be able to see if it has any impact on the hangs as well.

comments.author: Rothermel, Brent
comments.body:
Yesterday, Charles made a debug fm build for us that allowed us to pause the update of the LFT. We then ran a bounce test where we pause the LFT before enabling the port and then once the port was active we re enabled the LFT update. comments.author: Blocksome, Michael
comments.body:
I think the linux kernel will migrate the BTS thread to an unused core. But there is also a module parameter to specify the cpu:
One HFI
bulksvc_cpu=39
Two HFIs (unit0,unit1)bulksvc_cpu=39,71
Use -1 (or omit) to let the driver choose a CPU on the device’s NUMA node.
You can verify what’s loaded with:
cat /sys/module/hfi1/parameters/bulksvc_cpu
The parameter is created with S_IRUGO (read-only in sysfs), so you typically set it at module load time (modprobe / initramfs) rather than changing it live.
If you pick a CPU not in the HFI’s NUMA node, the driver will warn and performance may drop (the code explicitly checks and warns).

comments.author: Luick, Dean
comments.body:
I don’t know what Inventec’s issue is.  All we know is “PXE boot times out”.  That is not a lot of information.
The first order of business, when a timeout occurs, is to find out if the host was able to join the FM’s Multicast group.  This will give us some idea of whether the UEFI driver is able to send and receive packets with the FM.
Checking for multicast membership is time sensitive.  Run this command to ask the FM for the Multicast group memberships right after the timeout:
# check for multicast membership
opareport -o mcgroups
The PXE boot host will show up as “JKR MFG”.  If there are multiple hosts PXE booting, then a GUID match will need to be made.

comments.author: Luick, Dean
comments.body: How does Inventec test?  Do they have a host that is set up to automatically PXE boot?  Or do they manually PXE boot a host?  I ask because I am not aware that we test the automatic boot in-house.  I have asked that we set up something but nothing seems to have happened.

comments.author: Zhang, David

comments.author: Luick, Dean
comments.body:
Good to know that Inventec is manually PXE booting.  That matches what we are doing, at least.  What specific hosts are they testing?  E.g. Dell, Lenovo, SuperMicro, other?The thing would be to find out how far the UEFI driver got before it timed out.

The above can give clues as to how far the driver was able to progress.  If the driver is able to join Multicast groups, then it should be able to communicate normally and the issue would point to DHCP or tftp setup.
If there is no evidence that the driver was able to join a Multicast group, then we would need to run a debug UEFI driver.  The debug UEFI driver will print out a lot of information on the console, so a console text capture is needed.  Unfortunately, the debug UEFI driver prints so much stuff that it is unusable as a PXE boot client.  What I usually do is run it long enough to verify that the tftp has started (you can see repeated traffic on the tcpdump output).

comments.author: Luick, Dean
comments.body:
One additional item has come to my attention.  On the FM host / DHCPd host, make sure that only the active IPoIB device is “UP”.
For JKR, ipoib may create two devices.  For non-port-swapped cards, the the second device is the live device.  On port swapped cards, the first device is live.  The other is with the unused port.For example, in the following “ip -4 addr” output we see the two infiniband devices.
6: ibp33s0: <NO-CARRIER,BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP> mtu 10236 qdisc fq_codel state DOWN group default qlen 1000
    inet 192.168.2.5/24 brd 192.168.2.255 scope global noprefixroute ibp33s0
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
7: ibp33s0d1: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 2044 qdisc mq state UP group default qlen 1000
    inet 192.168.2.6/24 brd 192.168.2.255 scope global noprefixroute ibp33s0d1
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

In the flags in the <>, we see both are UP.  However, only ibp33s0d1 is the active device.
Both devices are on the same subnet: 192.168.2.5.The kernel may respond to ARP requests from the UEFI driver on the wrong device.  To ensure the correct device is used, make the unused ipoib device is “down” by this command.  Be sure to replace the device name used in the example with the local device name.
 ip link set dev ibp33s0 down
When looking at the devices again, we see that ibp33s0 is no longer “UP” in the <> brackets.
6: ibp33s0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST> mtu 10236 qdisc fq_codel state DOWN group default qlen 1000
    inet 192.168.2.5/24 brd 192.168.2.255 scope global noprefixroute ibp33s0
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
7: ibp33s0d1: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 2044 qdisc mq state UP group default qlen 1000
    inet 192.168.2.6/24 brd 192.168.2.255 scope global noprefixroute ibp33s0d1
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

I have another question:  When doing repeat PXE boot tests, how and when is the customer starting a new test?  My testing is showing that how I try to do successive PXE boots changes whether it works or not.
I see different results, depending on what I do between boot tests.  Let me give you an example.  This is how my setup works.  I suspect everyone’s setup can be different.
Starting from a booted OS:

Here is where things may be different.

At this point I can select the reboot or use ipmpitool to have the BMC (warm) reset the host

Next will be to try to understand why a boot select from the secondary grub menu doesn’t work.  My suspicion: The reboot selection doesn’t call a needed routine in UEFI that will notify our driver that it is done.  This, in turn, leaves the cport firmware thinking that the UEFI driver is still present.  However, the counter argument to this is that the system reboot should be telling the cport firmware (via the PERST# signal) that the OS booted, so it will reset everything internally anyway.

In the pass case, they will see grub menu and install OS, in a failed case, they will not get to the grub screen.

comments.author: Hebenstreit, Michael
comments.body:
question from NEC 
OPA FM config 
    <SwitchLifetime>33ms</SwitchLifetime>
    <HoqLife>8ms</HoqLife>
    <VLStallCount>7</VLStallCount>
The equivalent defaults in IB (nvidia NDR) are
# The code of maximal time a packet can wait at the head of
# transmission queue.
# The actual time is 4.096usec * 2^<head_of_queue_lifetime>
# The value 0x14 disables this mechanism
head_of_queue_lifetime 0x12



# The code of maximal time a packet can live in a switch
# The actual time is 4.096usec * 2^<packet_life_time>
# The value 0x14 disables this mechanism
packet_life_time 0x12

# The number of sequential packets dropped that cause the port
# to enter the VLStalled state. The result of setting this value to
# zero is undefined.
vl_stall_count 0x07

# The number of sequential packets dropped that cause the port
# to enter the VLStalled state. This value is for switch ports
# driving a CA or router port. The result of setting this value
# to zero is undefined.
leaf_vl_stall_count 0x07
this is 1s, compared to 8ms and 33ms.
NEC wonders if the timeout set for the ACK on the QP by IBM assumes something similar, and if a lower level rcresend is may be taking too long and triggers the higher layer timeout, when packets run into the lifetime restrictions waiting on congested ports.

comments.author: Hebenstreit, Michael
comments.body:
We have indications from a failed rping during a crash at Meteomatics that there IS a problem in the RDMA stack. 
Here is a timeline of the events. At 59 seconds we have an IBV_WC_RETRY_EXC_ERR  on 2 nodes mmstor13 and mmstor21. Both are trying to communicate with mmstor19. If I calculate 1.07s RDMA timeout times 6 retries the original message must have started around 52s. At 53s we had an rping test, and usually those tests are answered VERY quickly (less than 0.4s). That test fails, timing out at 59s. A little bit earlier at 50s the rping succeeds. It’s also interesting that the GPFS demon essentially goes to sleep at 51.5s and for the next 8s does not do much (until the RDMA qp closed message forces it to become active again). According to IBM that most likely reason this happens is that no messages are coming in. 
We need to figure out what can cause “error 28” and what we can do to collect more debug information during the next crash.

comments.author: Ozog, David
comments.body: This appears to be happening because the number of consumed user contexts is unexpectedly high on the nodes that are automatically disabling bulksvc.You can check how many contexts are available with this:cat /sys/class/infiniband/hfi1_0/ports/2/num_freectxtsIf there are not enough contexts available, then bulksvc will be disabled.I don’t understand why there are so many contexts consumed - below I see ~233 user contexts being used on the “bad” nodes, but 192 on the “good” nodes.  The benchmarks you are running should not be consuming so many contexts - at most 1 per Q,  if I’m not mistaken, so 4.A couple possibilities - There might be “zombie” processes on these nodes that are not being properly closed.  There also may have been issues with using “Control-C” to kill a job in process in previous versions, but I believe that issue should be resolved in newer versions of bulksvc. - Can you please check for which processes are consuming all the user contexts?  Thanks.
